snippet.title: Episode 356: HPE CEO Antonio Neri Cultivates a Culture of Innovation
snippet.description: Antonio Neri, President and CEO of Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE: HPE), is marking his fifth year helming the 60,000-employee company offering enterprise and cloud services and solutions. As both an engineer and an artist, Antonio’s career has taken him across the globe, from call center to CEO. He explains how HPE is continuing the purpose-driven ambitions that founders Bill Hewlett and David Packard instilled in HP’s DNA.  Antonio also shared his ambitions for HPE GreenLake to lead the edge-to-cloud sector by partnering with customers to meet future needs, from AI to supercomputing.

snippet.title: HPE CEO discusses 2024 outlook, says AI has as much potential as internet and smartphones
snippet.description:
Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) is leaning harder into its artificial intelligence commitments, viewing the industry as a $150 billion opportunity over the next three years alone. HPE CEO and President Antonio Neri sits down with Yahoo Finance Executive Editor Brian Sozzi to discuss the AI infrastructure the company is building up to keep up with customer demand.
"AI is moving much faster than the previous two cycles, whether it was the mobility access that everybody got or Web 1.0," Neri says, also commenting on employment concerns surrounding AI: "It provides the ability, for this generation, to learn in different ways and to do more value-added in their jobs," Neri says
Neri goes on to talk about the computer software maker's guidance and addresses concerns stemming from economic woes and geopolitical events.

snippet.title: How We Do It: Black Writers on Craft, Practice, and Skill
snippet.description:
About the book:
More than 30 acclaimed writers--including diverse voices such as Nikki Giovanni, David Omotosho Black, Natasha Trethewey, Barry Jenkins, Jacqueline Woodson, Tayari Jones, and Angela Flournoy--reflect on their experience and expertise in this unique book on the craft of writing that focuses on the Black creative spirit.
How We Do It is an anthology curated by Black writers for the creation and proliferation of Black thought. While a creator's ethnicity does not solely define them, it is inherently part of who they are and how they interpret the world.
For centuries, Black creators have utilized oral and written storytelling traditions in crafting their art. But how does one begin the process of constructing a poem or story or character? How do Black writers, when faced with questions of "authenticity," dive deep into the essence of their lives and work to find the inherent truth? How We Do It addresses these profound questions. Not a traditional "how to" writing handbook, it seeks to guide rather than dictate and to validate the complexity and range of styles--and even how one thinks about craft itself.
An outstanding list of contributors offer their insights on a range of important topics. Pulitzer Prize winner Jericho Brown explores the lives personified in poetry, while Pulitzer Prize winner Natasha Trethewey explores decolonizing enduring metaphors. National Book Award finalist Angela Flournoy illuminates the pain of grief in all forms and how it can be revealed in the act of creation, and iconoclast Nikki Giovanni offers an elegiac declaration on language.
New and previously published essays and interviews provide encouragement, examples, and templates, and offer lessons on everything from poetic form and plotting a story to the lessons inherent in the act of writing, trial & error, and finding inspiration in the works of others, including those of Toni Morrison, Shakespeare, and Edward P. Jones. A handbook and a reference tool, How We Do It is a thoughtful and welcome tool that offers direction to help Black artists establish their own creative practice while celebrating and widening the scope of the Black writer's role in art, history, and culture.
Contributors include Daniel Omotosho Black, Jericho Brown, Breena Clark, Rita Dove, Camille T. Dungy, W. Ralph Eubanks, Curdella Forbes, Angela Flournoy, Ernest Gaines, Nikki Giovanni, Marita Golden, Ravi Howard, Terrance Hayes, Mitchell S. Jackson, Barry Jenkins, Charles Johnson, Tayari Jones, Jamaica Kincaid, Tony Medina, E. Ethelbert Miller, Elizabeth Nunez, Carl Phillips, Jewell Parker Rhodes, Rion Amilcar Scott, Evie Shockley, Natasha Trethewey, Frank X Walker, Afaa M. Weaver, Crystal Wilkinson, Jacqueline Woodson, Tiphanie Yanique.
About the editors:
Darlene Taylor, a lecturer and co-faculty advisor to the Sterling A. Brown English Society at Howard University. She holds a BA from American University and an MFA from Stonecoast, University of Southern Maine and serves on its Writing for Social Justice Initiative. A cultural arts advocate, she founded INKPEN, a nonprofit dedicated to literary citizenship. A fellow of Callaloo and Kimbilio and former chair of the Zora Neale Hurston/Richard Wright Foundation, her writing explores little-known lives and histories. Taylor researches politics, historic preservation, cultural arts, and legacies handed-down from porch-talk storytellers. Her work appears in Idol Talk: Women Writers on the Teenage Infatuations That Changed Their Lives and KY Stories anthologies, Feminist Studies, Kweli Journal, Public History Commons, Portland Monthly and LA Parent.
About the host:
Khadijah Z. Ali-Coleman, Ed.D. is a cultural curator, community organizer, nationally recognized speaker, and writer. A playwright, she has had more than a dozen of her plays presented publicly in venues throughout the country, including the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, Theater Alliance, and Baltimore Theater Project. She was a Theater Alliance Quadrant Playwright from 2019-2021, a 2019 Fulbright-Hays scholar, a 2015 Maryland State Arts Council Independent Artist awardee and a 2012 Prince George’s County Social Innovation Fund Forty Under 40 awardee.
Marita Golden, cofounder and president emeritus of the Hurston/Wright Foundation, is a veteran teacher of writing and an acclaimed award-winning author of more than a dozen works of fiction and nonfiction. She has served as a member of the faculties of the MFA graduate creative writing programs at George Mason University and Virginia Commonwealth University and in the MA creative writing program at John Hopkins University and has taught writing internationally to a variety of constituencies. She has been interviewed by Oprah Winfrey as a remarkable leader for black women worldwide. She currently lives in Maryland.

She was a Theater Alliance Quadrant Playwright from 2019-2021, a 2019 Fulbright-Hays scholar, a 2015 Maryland State Arts Council Independent Artist awardee and a 2012 Prince George’s County Social Innovation Fund Forty Under 40 awardee.
Marita Golden, cofounder and president emeritus of the Hurston/Wright Foundation, is a veteran teacher of writing and an acclaimed award-winning author of more than a dozen works of fiction and nonfiction. She has served as a member of the faculties of the MFA graduate creative writing programs at George Mason University and Virginia Commonwealth University and in the MA creative writing program at John Hopkins University and has taught writing internationally to a variety of constituencies. She has been interviewed by Oprah Winfrey as a remarkable leader for black women worldwide. She currently lives in Maryland.

snippet.title: “There’s Only White People Because Black People Came First”
snippet.description:
#JaneElliott #BHM #AntiRacism #Shorts #JaneCrow

Speaker, educator, and activist Jane Elliott, known for her "Blue eyes/Brown eyes" exercise, joined us to talk about the role White women play in education and upholding racist systems and how to be an ally in the fight to dismantle them. Catch the interview here: https://youtu.be/v9S74fDOWLk

Jane Crow: Then and Now is a project that looks back in history to understand how (and why) white women as mothers and teachers have played a role in obstructing—and advancing—educational equity and how that history remains alive and active today. 

Learn more about the project here: https://www.edpost.com/jane-crow

snippet.title: DL Hughley: The Only Thing White Men in America Have Lost is Ability to Say the N-Word (Part 6)
snippet.description:
Watch the full interview now as a VladTV Youtube Member - https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCg7lal8IC-xPyKfgH4rdUcA/join

Part 5: https://youtu.be/2vsqoOP5-PU
Part 7: https://youtu.be/8rmQYia-sU0
Part 1: https://youtu.be/RkxwzMcg-pY
--------
In this clip, Vlad continued to speak about Scott Adams, who said that his Black friends would support his racist comments. D.L. then spoke about how the only repercussions that white men have faced for saying derogatory things is not being able to say the n-word. Vlad also spoke to D.L. about Roland Martin saying that white people aren’t optimistic for the future, and D.L. stated that a time capsule for white people would go back to the 1950s, while one for Black people would go back to 2008. Moving along, Vlad spoke about his conversation with Scott Adams, who asked why Vlad lives where he lives. This led to D.L. stating Black and Hispanic people were told where to live, and he added that no housing development has ever been built for them, aside from prisons.

snippet.title: HVAC Professionals! Understanding  Psychrometrics is key to Controlling Temperature and Humidity
snippet.description:
When we moved to the mountains of Western North Carolina, we had no idea we were moving into a rainforest.

So, the mountains here are classified as a Temperate Rainforest. It's an excellent place to study and experience 100% saturation on the psychrometric chart!

YouTube Channel:
https://www.youtube.com/@HVAC-TV
The Engineers HVAC Podcast:
https://anchor.fm/engineers-hvac-podcast
Connect with me on LinkedIn:
https://www.linkedin.com/in/tony-mormino
Insight Partners (Commercial HVAC Products in NC, SC, GA): Website: https://www.insightusa.com/
Hobbs & Assocaited, Inc. (Commercial HVAC Products in VA, TN, MD, AL): https://hobbsassociates.com/

A temperate rainforest is a forest that receives high levels of rainfall, typically over 60 inches per year. The climate is cool and mild year-round, with temperatures ranging from 40 to 60 degrees Fahrenheit. The trees in a temperate rainforest are typically evergreen, such as spruce, fir, and hemlock. The understory is often lush and mossy, with various ferns, wildflowers, and other plants.

Facts:
- The temperate rainforests of western North Carolina are located in the high mountains of the Appalachian Mountains.
- The highest peak in North Carolina, Mount Mitchell, is in a temperate rainforest.
- The temperate rainforests of western North Carolina are home to various wildlife, including black bears, deer, salamanders, and birds.

Here are some other interesting facts about temperate rainforests in western North Carolina:
- They are home to a variety of rare and endangered species.
- The fog that often hangs in the trees helps to keep the air moist and cool.
- The trees in a temperate rainforest are adapted to the cool, wet climate.
- They have thick bark to protect them from the elements, and their leaves are often small and needle-like to help them shed water.
- Temperate rainforests play an essential role in the water cycle. They help regulate water flow and provide a habitat for many different plants and animals.

#hvac #mechanicalegineering #mechanicalengineer #hvactechnician #hvaclife #psychrometrics #fog

snippet.title: STOP Fantasy Booking Sami Zayn to Headline WrestleMania 39 | Sami Zayn Turns on the Bloodline
snippet.description:
STOP Fantasy Booking Sami Zayn to Headline WrestleMania 39 | Sami Zayn Turns on the Bloodline 
#WWE #RoyalRumble #CodyRhodes #SamiZayn 
0:00 Sami Zayn turns on the Bloodline, Super Bowl LVII 
2:01 Fans Fantasy Booking of Sami Zayn  
4:11 LetsGetChecked Ad Read
5:57 WWE Booking in Triple H Era has Improved 
8:36 Entitled Fans Booking WrestleMania 39 need to Shut Up 

Video courtesy of BT Sport https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjaRMNtXOrc
Go to our sponsor http://trylgc.com/ProWrestlingBits and use the code Wrestling25 for 25% off your LetsGetChecked home test kit. 

The Bloodline made another masterpiece at he Royal Rumble with zero input from fantasy booking, yet fans are still trying to book Sami Zayn for WrestleMania. 

Wow, you're in the mix with Pro Wrestling Bits! 

WWE Royal Rumble 2023 ended with one of the greatest angles in WWE history. The type of angle that had once-in-a-generation heat, whether it was the decible-breaking pop for Sami Zayn standing up to Roman Reigns, or the deafening chants of "F—k you Roman” from the San Antonio Alamodome. God if great sex was a storyline I'd still be having a cigarette after the Royal Rumble. WWE has created a Bloodline universe, with no less than six deeply fleshed out characters. Internal emotional conflicts, like Jey Uso's, can be understood with a simple facial expression. 

The Bloodline is a story of power, loyalty and brotherhood. Speaking of which, quick sidebar, the Super Bowl will pit the Kansas Chiefs against The Philadelphia Eagles, and it'll be the first time ever two brothers play against each other in a Super Bowl. Those brothers, of course, are Pat Mahomes and Jalen Hurts. Because this will be the first Super Bowl with two Black quarterbacks. Smack dab in the middle of Black History Month. How many Super Bowl parties will be serving sweet potato pie this year? The media has already fallen in love with the Kelce vs. Kelce narrative, but with all this Black history being made at Super Bowl LVII, let's not just talk about the white brothers playing in this game. 

Anyway, Sami Zayn has been a great White brother to The Bloodline as its Honorary Uce. When all is said and done, this might be the best storyline WWE ever did in the modern era. 

If Hollywood wasn't so obsessed with showering sexual predators with its awards and accolades, they'd consider WWE's Bloodline storyline for an Emmy. By the way, now that Vince McMahon is back, shouldn't that give WWE more of a chance to win an Emmy given how much alleged sexual predators are celebrated in Hollywood? More on WrestleMania Hollywood very soon, and I can't wait.

-Headed into the Royal Rumble, Sami Zayn was a trendy pick to win it all. Shit, I picked Sami to win the Royal Rumble. In fact, fantasy booking got so out of control that Zayn addressed it in an interview with Ariel Helwani. 

"Maybe you and I are looking at it differently because we're die hards, but I think there are a lot of casual fans who are along for the ride." 

Exactly. In fact, Sami was 100% right. Because just like Daniel Bryan in 2014, Sami wasn't even in the Royal Rumble. Unlike 2014 though, fans were perfectly fine with that as they cheered Cody Rhodes to his first Royal Rumble win, LET'S GO CODY!! 

WWE live events have been selling out week after week, month after month. And while its TV audience is mostly hardcore, the people going to these events are not the entitled Internet Wrestling Fans from a decade ago who hijacked shows. The Alamodome was filled with a WWE-friendly crowd that just want to be entertained. The best kind of a wrestling crowd. Therefore, they just let WWE cook, and even though WWE's plans did not align with all the crazy hypotheticals floating around, the payoffs were better than anybody could have ever imagined, and there were no chants of "We Want Rocky" or "We Want Sami."

Headed into the Rumble, there was all kinds of speculation and demands, like how Sami MUST win the Royal Rumble, or that Austin might show up. Or how Dwayne might show up. None of that happened. But The Bloodline still had an all-time segment. What does that tell you? 

WWE's job is not to listen to the fans. That's bullshit.  Listen to the fans long enough and you'll be sitting with them. WWE did not assemble an entire team of writers and some of the best wrestling minds in the business, for Twitter to book its storylines. WWE's job is to understand its fanbase, so even if its plans don't line up with fantasy booking, they still lead to great payoffs, not because WWE listened to its fans, but because WWE understands what will make them happy. Lucky for you, I know exactly what will make you happy, a word from our sponsors at LetsGetChecked.

-Headed into the Royal Rumble, Sami Zayn was a trendy pick to win it all. Shit, I picked Sami to win the Royal Rumble. In fact, fantasy booking got so out of control that Zayn addressed it in an interview with Ariel Helwani. 

"Maybe you and I are looking at it differently because we're die hards, but I think there are a lot of casual fans who are along for the ride." 

Exactly. In fact, Sami was 100% right. Because just like Daniel Bryan in 2014, Sami wasn't even in the Royal Rumble. Unlike 2014 though, fans were perfectly fine with that as they cheered Cody Rhodes to his first Royal Rumble win, LET'S GO CODY!! 

WWE live events have been selling out week after week, month after month. And while its TV audience is mostly hardcore, the people going to these events are not the entitled Internet Wrestling Fans from a decade ago who hijacked shows. The Alamodome was filled with a WWE-friendly crowd that just want to be entertained. The best kind of a wrestling crowd. Therefore, they just let WWE cook, and even though WWE's plans did not align with all the crazy hypotheticals floating around, the payoffs were better than anybody could have ever imagined, and there were no chants of "We Want Rocky" or "We Want Sami."

Headed into the Rumble, there was all kinds of speculation and demands, like how Sami MUST win the Royal Rumble, or that Austin might show up. Or how Dwayne might show up. None of that happened. But The Bloodline still had an all-time segment. What does that tell you? 

WWE's job is not to listen to the fans. That's bullshit.  Listen to the fans long enough and you'll be sitting with them. WWE did not assemble an entire team of writers and some of the best wrestling minds in the business, for Twitter to book its storylines. WWE's job is to understand its fanbase, so even if its plans don't line up with fantasy booking, they still lead to great payoffs, not because WWE listened to its fans, but because WWE understands what will make them happy.
